title: Robustness of fiber-optic attenuators to 1061-nm sub-nanosecond pulsed laser radiation in quantum key distribution systems
authors: Daria Ruzhitskaya, Irina Zhluktova, Anastasiya Ponosova, Fedor Ushakov, Andrey Zverev, Galina Tertyshnikova, Tianyi Xing, Kirill Min'kov, Daniil Trefilov, Anqi Huang, Vladimir Kamynin, Vladimir Tsvetkov, Vadim Makarov
abstract: The security of quantum key distribution (QKD) systems relies on the physical integrity of their components. While laser-damage attacks (LDAs) using high-power continuous-wave (cw) lasers have been well studied, the threat posed by pulsed lasers at alternative wavelengths remains underestimated. Here, we experimentally investigated the stability of four types of fiber-optic attenuators under exposure to sub-picosecond pulses at 1061 nm with average power reaching 1 W. Mechanical variable attenuators with blocking elements and fixed air-gap attenuators show resistance to this attack. MEMS-based variable attenuators exhibit increased attenuation or irreversible damage that causes a permanent reduction in attenuation of approximately 3.8 dB. For fixed attenuators with an absorption element, we demonstrate that initial pulsed irradiation significantly lowers the optical damage threshold of the components compared to direct cw attacks. The attenuation reduction achieved is up to 7 dB at a 1 W cw laser at 1550 nm. These results highlight the possibility of establishing a hidden side-channel for eavesdropping attacks and underscore the insufficiency of existing countermeasures against sophisticated LDA scenarios.
summary: This paper investigates how high-power pulsed lasers at 1061 nm can damage or 'prime' optical components in QKD systems. The researchers found that MEMS-based attenuators can suffer permanent damage, and that pulsed light can lower the damage threshold of fixed attenuators for subsequent continuous-wave attacks. These findings reveal a potential hidden side-channel for eavesdropping that existing countermeasures might not address.
main_problem: Investigating the vulnerability of fiber-optic attenuators in QKD systems to laser-damage attacks (LDAs) using 1061-nm sub-nanosecond pulsed lasers, which may create hidden side-channels.
main_result: MEMS-based attenuators suffered irreversible damage (up to 3.8 dB reduction), and pulsed irradiation was found to 'prime' absorption-based fixed attenuators, significantly lowering their optical damage threshold for subsequent CW attacks.
method: Experimental exposure of four types of fiber-optic attenuators to 1061-nm pulsed laser radiation (up to 1 W average power) and monitoring changes in 1550-nm transmission, accompanied by microscopic and spectroscopic analysis.
model_or_system: An experimental setup involving a 1061-nm pulsed laser (master oscillator and YDFA stages) and a 1550-nm DFB laser simulating QKD signals, testing various attenuators including MEMS-based, mechanical, and fixed-gap/absorption types.
key_observables: Attenuation coefficient changes at 1550 nm, transmission spectra (1061 nm vs 1550 nm), surface temperature, and microscopic damage patterns.
important_parameters: 1061-nm wavelength, 1550-nm operating wavelength, 1 MHz repetition rate, 260-410 ps pulse duration, and laser average power up to 1030 mW.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the observed attenuation changes (reduction >= 1 dB or increase >= 3 dB) are sufficient to constitute a successful eavesdropping or denial-of-service attack.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Experimental setup schematic; Fig 2: Laser power vs. pulse duration; Fig 3: Transmission spectra comparison; Fig 4: Mechanical VOA photo; Fig 5-7: MEMS VOA attenuation changes and damage; Fig 8: Microscopic damage comparison.
paper_structure: Introduction of the LDA threat, description of the experimental setup and laser parameters, results for different attenuator types (mechanical, MEMS, fixed), analysis of damage mechanisms (carbonization/priming), and conclusions on security implications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental setup. DFB, 1550-nm distributed- feedback laser; WDM, wavelength-division multiplexer; PL, pulsed laser; TC, thermocouple fixed on the attenuator hous- ing. Light directions with minimal loss in setup are denoted by green (light gray) for 1061 nm and red (dark gray) for 1550 nm.
FIG. 2. The dependence of average output power and pulse duration of PL in single-pulse regime. Inset shows the pulse shape.

FIG. 3. Initial transmission spectra of the testing attenua- tors. The spectra show the intrinsic attenuation profile of each sample. The red and green vertical lines denote the operating wavelength of the QKD system and the central wavelength of the PL used in tests, respectively.
FIG. 4. Manual VOA with blocking element. (a) Simplified cross-section (not to scale). Red arrows denote light propa- gation direction. (b) Photograph of tested sample.
FIG. 6. Variation of the MEMS attenuation coefficient (black line) depending on the incident power of PL (red or gray line) in single-pulse regime.
FIG. 5. MEMS variable optical attenuator. (a) Simplified cross-section showing main elements (not to scale). Red ar- rows denote light propagation direction. (b) Photograph of the test sample.
FIG. 7. Alteration in the attenuation of MEMS VOA follow- ing testing in the multi-pulse exposure regime.

FIG. 8. Internal MEMS components after attack by (a) cw laser (damaged in [11]) and (b) PL. 1, ferrule; 2, glue; 3, glass collar. Darkening area indicates damage after exposure to PL. (c) Optical microscope images of the damaged area after exposure to PL.

FIG. 9. Fixed attenuator with gap-loss. (a) Simplified cross- section (not to scale). Red arrows denote light propagation direction. (b) Photograph of the attenuator. (c) The internal elements under the section of metal housing.

FIG. 10. Fixed attenuator with absorption element. (a) Sim- plified cross-section (not to scale). Red arrows denote light propagation direction. (b) Photograph of the attenuator. (c) The internal component with the metal housing removed. (d) Magnified view of one of the ferrules with the absorbing element. (e) Optical microscope (Altami SM 1065) image of the absorbing element’s tip. (f) Scanning electron microscope (Quattro S) image of the same tip. Red triangles mark the areas where elemental analysis was performed.
